abstract:
In recent years, scholars have increasingly scrutinized the democratic implications of "emergency politics" in the European Union, arguing that the persistent use of executive maneuvers to address crises—such as the Eurozone and migration challenges—undermines deliberation and may provoke a Eurosceptic backlash. This paper investigates whether the public actually perceives these emergency modes of governance as disempowering. Utilizing the COVID-19 pandemic as a critical case of a simultaneous and symmetrical transnational crisis, we analyze survey data from 15 EU member states to evaluate public opinion across four dimensions of emergency rule: policy uniformity (script), speed of execution, power asymmetries (size), and clarity of accountability.

Our analysis contrasts the theoretical expectations of emergency politics scholarship with those of transnational cleavage theory. The findings reveal that widespread public disillusionment with EU emergency governance is largely absent. Instead, perceptions of crisis management are robustly structured by citizens' prior attitudes toward European integration and their ideological leanings. Notably, "national-communitarian" voters express greater concern over the erosion of national autonomy, while "cosmopolitan-universalist" voters remain more supportive, suggesting that the integration-demarcation cleavage remains the primary driver of Euroscepticism. Ultimately, this research indicates that while emergency politics may bypass traditional democratic procedures, it has not significantly alienated the broader European public, as evaluations of crisis governance continue to be filtered through existing political identities rather than serving as a novel source of anti-system sentiment.
